one_line_takeaway: ARM shows, via a controlled 3-scenario differential test, that representing denied tool calls as first-class provenance nodes with counterfactual edges to the next action lets a graph-aware enforcement layer catch a denial-feedback exfiltration pattern (plus transitive taint and mixed-field leaks) that a flat citation-based provenance check misses entirely, at sub-millisecond overhead -- but the evaluation is a small hand-built proof of concept, not a benchmark-scale security study.
key_finding: On all three constructed attack scenarios, the flat citation-based baseline (L2) allowed the attack (Allow) while the graph-aware engine (L2G) denied it (Deny), per Table 2: (1) Causality laundering / denial inference exfiltration -- read_file('/etc/shadow') denied by Layer 1 (HB-2 sensitive path), followed by send_email(body='shadow file exists') composed by the LLM from the denial signal (14 characters, no credential patterns, no returned-data lineage): flat = Allow, graph-aware = Deny via detection of a Counterfactual edge from the denied read_file to the send_email call. (2) Transitive taint chain -- untrusted read_emails() output laundered through an intermediate format_response() call before reaching send_email() to finance@company.com: flat = Allow (finds format_response's output in history, citation check passes), graph-aware = Deny (MinTrust traced back to ToolUntrusted origin via graph reachability). (3) Mixed-provenance field exploit -- get_contact() returns a structured object with a trusted name field and an untrusted email field, then send_email(to=<untrusted email>): flat = Allow (treats the whole object as one citable unit), graph-aware = Deny (field-level provenance resolves the email field's trust independently). The full enforcement pipeline (L1+L2G+L3+L4) executed in under 1 ms for all three scenarios, described by the authors as negligible relative to typical LLM inference latency (100 ms to several seconds).
core_contribution: The paper identifies and formalizes 'causality laundering': a denial-feedback leakage pattern in which an LLM tool-calling agent, after a protected tool call is denied, infers security-relevant information from the denial outcome (e.g., 'permission denied' vs 'file not found') and exfiltrates that inference through a later, superficially benign tool call that has no direct data-flow lineage to the denied action. The paper's own artifact is ARM (Agentic Reference Monitor), a deterministic runtime enforcement layer/MCP proxy that mediates every tool call through a 4-layer policy pipeline (Hard Boundaries, Graph-Aware Provenance, Schema-Derived Constraints, Manual Policy) built around a provenance graph with four node types (VCall, VData, VDataField, VDeniedAction) and four edge types (DirectOutput, InputTo, FieldOf, Counterfactual). Denied actions become first-class provenance nodes; the next tool call automatically receives a Counterfactual edge from the denial node (a conservative temporal-adjacency heuristic), enabling detection of denial-induced causal influence in addition to transitive taint propagation and field-level trust tracking.
threat_model: Single-agent deployment where an LLM interacts with the world exclusively via tool calls (e.g., MCP), mediated by ARM at every invocation. Trusted inputs: system prompt (SysInstr) and user messages (UserInput); tool outputs range from ToolTrusted to ToolUntrusted, with ToolDesc (MCP tool metadata) least trusted, forming a 5-level total-order integrity lattice (ToolDesc < ToolUntrusted < ToolTrusted < UserInput < SysInstr). The attacker acts indirectly by controlling content the agent observes (e.g., injected prompt-injection payloads in tool outputs, manipulated MCP tool descriptions/'rug-pull' schema changes) but cannot modify agent code, ARM itself, or the system prompt. Attacker capabilities explicitly enumerated: (1) inject adversarial content into tool outputs, (2) craft multi-step attack chains that launder provenance through intermediate tool calls, (3) exploit denial signals by inferring information from a blocked call and exfiltrating that inference, (4) manipulate tool descriptions to steer tool selection/argument construction. Malicious end users are explicitly out of scope; the paper focuses on indirect compromise via untrusted tool-observed content in a single-agent setting (multi-agent extension deferred to future work). Security goals formalized as three properties: Mediated Integrity (Theorem 1: no causal path from untrusted source to an Allow verdict on a privileged sink without traversing an enforcement check), Tamper-Evident Audit (hash-chained log), and Defense in Depth (independent layers in series).
limitations: The authors state these explicitly (Sections 7.6 and 9): the flat baseline is a minimal, intentionally weak flat-history mechanism constructed to isolate the graph model's representational contribution -- it is NOT a reimplementation of FIDES, PCAS, or other cited full systems, so the comparison should not be read as 'ARM beats FIDES/PCAS empirically.' The evaluation uses only 3 manually constructed scenarios, not an automated benchmark suite (AgentDojo, InjectAgent named as future work); it does not measure false-positive rates, coverage across diverse agentic workflows, or behavior under longer provenance histories, and there is no benchmark-scale end-to-end experiment with frontier LLMs in the loop. The core counterfactual-edge mechanism links a denial only to the immediately following tool call -- a deliberately conservative temporal-adjacency heuristic (not true causal inference, since the LLM's internal reasoning is opaque) that can both over-approximate (false positives on benign calls that merely follow a denial) and under-approximate (miss delayed or multi-step laundering chains spread across several intervening actions). Field-level protection only helps if integrators supply meaningful per-field trust overrides; if all fields inherit the parent object's trust, field-level provenance collapses to ordinary object-level tainting. Complete mediation (Theorem 1) depends on an operational assumption -- that all tool calls are actually routed through the ARM proxy -- which code alone cannot guarantee. The system does not claim full non-interference for implicit flows generally (e.g., timing channels), only mitigation of a specific denial-feedback class. Declassification (upgrading a value's trust after validation) is identified as an open problem, not solved. Multi-agent extension is explicitly deferred to future work.

quantitative_results: Table 2: flat baseline (L2) misses all 3 attacks (causality laundering, transitive taint chain, mixed-provenance field exploit) = Allow in all cases; graph-aware engine (L2G) blocks all 3 = Deny in all cases
Full enforcement pipeline (L1+L2G+L3+L4) executes in under 1 ms for all three scenarios, median of 100 runs per scenario after warm-up, on Apple M4 Pro / 48 GB RAM

Enforcement codebase: ~910 lines total (arm_core: PolicyEngine/LayeredPolicyEngine ~130, CapabilityToken/ToolPermission ~50, L1 HardBoundariesLayer ~120, L2 flat baseline ~110, L3 SchemaDerivedLayer ~80, L4 ManualPolicyLayer ~60, AuditLog ~60, MCP Proxy ~50; arm_provenance: ProvenanceGraph ~430, GraphProvenanceLayer/L2G ~60, GraphAwareEngine ~110), plus ~600 lines of test code
45 unit tests total: 34 for provenance graph construction/trust lattice/reachability/counterfactual edges, 11 for graph-aware engine end-to-end scenarios
5-level integrity lattice: ToolDesc < ToolUntrusted < ToolTrusted < UserInput < SysInstr
Hard Boundary rule HB-1: string arguments over 10,000 characters denied; HB-3: any tool called more than 100 times per session denied
baselines_compared: Flat baseline (L2): a citation-based provenance layer implemented within ARM itself (checks only whether an argument value appears somewhere in the agent's prior tool-call history) -- NOT a reimplementation of FIDES, PCAS, or CaMeL; explicitly a minimal internal control condition to isolate the graph-aware layer's added value, per the paper's own stated limitation
